quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Environmental justice"?

options.text: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, or income in environmental decisions, codified by Executive Order 12898 (1994)
A population maintaining relatively constant size around carrying capacity through density-dependent regulation
The total variety of genes and alleles within a population; higher diversity increases resilience to environmental change
A chemical used to kill unwanted organisms (insects, weeds, pathogens), with potential environmental persistence and ecosystem impacts

quiz_questions.explanation: "Environmental justice" is defined as: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, or income in environmental decisions, codified by Executive Order 12898 (1994). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sacrifice zone"?

options.text: Pollutant directly emitted to atmosphere (vehicle exhaust, industrial emissions)
The pathway of a chemical element or nutrient through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem
A symbiotic relationship where a parasite benefits by reducing host fitness; can be a significant limiting factor
Geographic area permanently degraded by heavy pollution or industrial activity, disproportionately located in low-income communities and communities of color

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sacrifice zone" is defined as: Geographic area permanently degraded by heavy pollution or industrial activity, disproportionately located in low-income communities and communities of color. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Environmental racism"?

options.text: Systemic placement of hazardous facilities (landfills, refineries, incinerators) in communities of color and low-income areas
Fraction of soil contamination capable of being absorbed by organisms, depending on contaminant form and soil properties
Mass of material extracted to meet human consumption, greater than direct material input due to supply chain waste
Nine sustainability thresholds for earth system processes (climate, biodiversity, nutrient cycles, freshwater, land use, etc.) beyond which risk increases substantially

quiz_questions.explanation: "Environmental racism" is defined as: Systemic placement of hazardous facilities (landfills, refineries, incinerators) in communities of color and low-income areas. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cumulative impact assessment"?

options.text: Continued population growth despite replacement fertility, from age structure weighted toward reproductive years
Distinct soil layers with different characteristics reflecting weathering depth, organic matter content, and leaching patterns
Analysis of combined health and environmental effects from multiple pollution sources in a community, revealing disparities invisible when evaluating facilities individually
Extended period of abnormally low rainfall, leading to water shortage and environmental degradation

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cumulative impact assessment" is defined as: Analysis of combined health and environmental effects from multiple pollution sources in a community, revealing disparities invisible when evaluating facilities individually. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Climate justice"?

options.text: Point of maximum extraction rate followed by decline, already occurring for conventional oil and some mineral resources
Recognition that vulnerable populations experience disproportionate climate impacts despite minimal greenhouse gas emissions
Average number of children per woman; replacement level is ~2.1 in developed countries, <2.0 in many developed countries
A population maintaining relatively constant size around carrying capacity through density-dependent regulation

quiz_questions.explanation: "Climate justice" is defined as: Recognition that vulnerable populations experience disproportionate climate impacts despite minimal greenhouse gas emissions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, or income in environmental decisions, codified by Executive Order 12898 (1994)"?

options.text: Environmental justice
Stockholm Convention
Halons
Soil degradation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Environmental justice".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Geographic area permanently degraded by heavy pollution or industrial activity, disproportionately located in low-income communities and communities of color"?

options.text: Water quality standard
Sacrifice zone
Soil acidification
Monoculture

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sacrifice zone".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Systemic placement of hazardous facilities (landfills, refineries, incinerators) in communities of color and low-income areas"?

options.text: Environmental racism
Methane
Persistent organic pollutant (POP)
Erosion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Environmental racism".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Analysis of combined health and environmental effects from multiple pollution sources in a community, revealing disparities invisible when evaluating facilities individually"?

options.text: Cumulative impact assessment
Energy efficiency
Parasitism
Restoration

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cumulative impact assessment".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Recognition that vulnerable populations experience disproportionate climate impacts despite minimal greenhouse gas emissions"?

options.text: Fishing down the food chain
Facilitation
Climate justice
Capacity factor

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Climate justice".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Global Environmental Justice is accurate?

options.text: Water Pollution Types and Sources degrade drinking water quality, aquatic ecosystem integrity, and human health through point sources (factories, wastewater plants) and nonpoint sources (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater) introducing...
Disruptions to nutrient cycles through industrial processes, agriculture, and combustion have fundamentally altered planetary biogeochemistry and created significant environmental challenges.

Global Environmental Justice addresses disproportionate exposure of low-income communities and communities of color to pollution, toxic facilities, and environmental hazards while having less access to environmental amenities and political power...
Freshwater and marine ecosystems receive approximately 3.5 billion tons of wastewater annually, with developing countries treating <10% before discharge.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Environmental Justice as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Environmental racism—deliberate placement of polluting facilities near marginalized communities—has created health disparities in air quality, water contamination, soil pollution, and climate impacts affecting these populations most severely...

Nuclear Energy generates electricity through controlled fission of uranium-235 or plutonium-239, providing low-carbon, high-density energy but creating long-lived radioactive waste requiring secure isolation for millennia.

Indoor pollutant concentrations often exceed outdoor levels due to concentrated sources (tobacco, appliances, building materials) and insufficient ventilation, particularly in energy-efficient buildings.
Sustainable fisheries management requires assessing stock status, setting science-based catch limits, and protecting critical habitats.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Environmental Justice as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Density-dependent regulation occurs through increased mortality, decreased reproduction, or emigration as populations increase, creating stable equilibrium populations.
Environmental justice requires equitable decision-making, remediation of existing injustices, and prevention of future impacts.
Population Age Structure describes the distribution of individuals across age classes, and fundamentally determines population growth rates, migration potential, and demographic projections.
Climate (30-year average weather) results from atmospheric, oceanic, and cryospheric interactions influenced increasingly by human greenhouse gas emissions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Environmental Justice as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Global Environmental Justice addresses disproportionate exposure of low-income communities and communities of color to pollution, toxic facilities, and environmental hazards while having less access to environmental amenities and political power...

Soil Fertility and Erosion describe soil's capacity to provide available nutrients for plant growth and the detachment and transport of soil by water and wind, which threatens global food security by removing productive topsoil accumulated over...
Population growth exacerbates resource scarcity, environmental impact, and inequality, requiring family planning, education (especially female), and economic development.
Successful restoration requires understanding the original ecosystem's composition, structure, and ecological processes, then removing limiting factors and facilitating species reestablishment.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Environmental Justice as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Energy Flow Through Ecosystems involves the one-directional movement of energy from the sun through producers, consumers, and decomposers, with approximately 10% of energy transferred between each trophic level.
Understanding weather patterns is essential for agriculture, disaster preparedness, and predicting climate change impacts.

Environmental racism—deliberate placement of polluting facilities near marginalized communities—has created health disparities in air quality, water contamination, soil pollution, and climate impacts affecting these populations most severely...

Soil Fertility and Erosion describe soil's capacity to provide available nutrients for plant growth and the detachment and transport of soil by water and wind, which threatens global food security by removing productive topsoil accumulated over...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Global Environmental Justice as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Implementing marine protected areas in hypoxic regions to prevent bottom trawling that resuspends anoxic sediments and prevents recovery
Remediating lead contamination in low-income neighborhoods through soil remediation, housing rehabilitation, and water system replacement
Protecting coral reef ecosystems through MPAs that prevent fishing-related habitat destruction and allow reef fish recovery
Monitoring atmospheric CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion using ice cores and continuous monitoring to inform climate policy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Monitoring freshwater aquifer depletion rates (Ogallala, Arabian, North China Plain) to prevent permanent loss of irrigation capacity
Siting environmental facilities (wind farms, solar arrays, waste facilities) through inclusive processes ensuring community benefit and addressing historical inequities
Monitoring methane leakage from natural gas infrastructure to quantify climate impacts and identify equipment upgrades reducing emissions
Analyzing how wolf predation limits elk population growth in Yellowstone, affecting vegetation recovery and demonstrating top-down control

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Global Environmental Justice applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Using harvest control rules to adjust catch limits in response to fish stock assessments, preventing predictable collapses of groundfish fisheries
Controlling invasive carp populations in North American lakes through manipulation of recruitment drivers and commercial harvesting
Reducing power plant SO₂ emissions through fuel switching to low-sulfur coal, coal desulfurization, or renewable energy transition
Advancing climate adaptation in vulnerable communities (low-income, indigenous) through participatory planning, green infrastructure investment, and resilience building

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Global Environmental Justice?

options.text: Monitoring freshwater aquifer depletion rates (Ogallala, Arabian, North China Plain) to prevent permanent loss of irrigation capacity
Controlling invasive carp populations in North American lakes through manipulation of recruitment drivers and commercial harvesting
Siting large wind farms to maximize resource utilization while minimizing impacts on bird migration and local landscapes
Remediating lead contamination in low-income neighborhoods through soil remediation, housing rehabilitation, and water system replacement

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Global Environmental Justice in practice?

options.text: Siting environmental facilities (wind farms, solar arrays, waste facilities) through inclusive processes ensuring community benefit and addressing historical inequities
Understanding snowshoe hare-lynx population cycles where predator-prey dynamics create oscillations through predator lagging and prey recovery
Supporting agroforestry systems integrating trees with crops to improve soil quality, increase biodiversity, and enhance farmer resilience
Assessing the carrying capacity of rangelands by measuring primary productivity and determining sustainable livestock stocking rates

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Global Environmental Justice.
